guides.title: Zakat Eligibility: What It Means and How We Handle It
guides.description: The 8 asnaf (zakat recipient categories), scholarly considerations around programs vs. overhead, and how Good Measure Giving flags zakat-accepting charities without making the fiqh call for you.

guides.title: How to Evaluate a Muslim Charity
guides.description: A practical five-question framework: programs, financials, evidence of impact, governance, and the zakat dimension — plus the traps (like the overhead myth) that mislead well-intentioned donors.

guides.title: The Top Zakat-Eligible Charities of 2026
guides.description: The ten highest-scoring US charities that publicly accept zakat — grouped by cause area, with high data confidence behind every score. Refreshed annually before Ramadan.

guides.title: Sadaqah vs Zakat: What's Actually Different
guides.description: Zakat is an obligation with fixed rules — nisab, 2.5%, eight recipient categories, intention. Sadaqah is voluntary and unrestricted. What follows from that difference, practically.

guides.title: The Nisab Explained: How Much Wealth Triggers Zakat?
guides.description: The zakat threshold: 85 grams of gold or 595 grams of silver, where those numbers come from, why they diverge so sharply today, and how the threshold interacts with your zakat year.

guides.title: The Complete Zakat Planning Guide
guides.description: A six-step system: fix your anniversary date, inventory zakatable assets, subtract liabilities, calculate, plan the distribution, and keep records that make next year take twenty minutes.

guides.title: How to Budget Your Giving: A Muslim Donor's Playbook
guides.description: Zakat as the floor, a chosen sadaqah rate, four buckets, automation, and the seasonal moments that multiply reward — a structure you decide once a year.

guides.title: Who Receives Zakat? The 8 Categories (Asnaf)
guides.description: The Qur'an restricts zakat to eight categories of recipients. What each means, its modern applications — refugees, trafficking victims, medical debt — and where scholars disagree.
